negativeFixture.description: Technically valid and editorially coherent, but dominated by a repeated headline-body-three-points silhouette with limited real visual evidence.

briefs.title: RelayOS Series A Roadshow
briefs.decision: decide whether RelayOS has enough traction and technical defensibility for diligence
briefs.prompt: Create an evidence-led seven-slide Series A roadshow for early-stage enterprise software investors deciding whether RelayOS merits diligence. Use only the attached operating metrics and product facts. Show the workflow problem, product transformation, traction, market wedge, defensible architecture, financing use, and a clear diligence decision. Include an editable traction chart, an editable system architecture diagram, a large honestly labeled product screenshot slot, and one restrained market comparison. Do not invent customers, market size, quotes, or growth rates.

RelayOS coordinates approval-heavy operations. The product keeps proposals reviewable before mutation, records versioned receipts, and exposes adapters for CRM, ticketing, and document systems. No product screenshot was supplied to the benchmark.

briefs.title: Adaptive Retrieval Under Cost Constraints
briefs.decision: understand whether the adaptive policy improves answer quality per dollar
briefs.prompt: Create a seven-slide research talk for applied machine-learning researchers evaluating adaptive retrieval under cost constraints. Explain the question, method, evaluation design, result, failure modes, and implications. Use the supplied experiment table exactly. Include an editable methods diagram, an editable result chart, the cost-adjusted quality formula Q divided by cost, and claim-adjacent source labels. Separate measured results from interpretation.

attachments.content: The adaptive policy predicts whether a query requires retrieval, retrieves at most three passages, then answers with claim-linked citations. Reported values are means over the fixed benchmark fixture; no statistical-significance claim is authorized.

briefs.title: Portable Caseflow Runtime
briefs.decision: approve the boundary between portable protocol and backend adapters
briefs.prompt: Create a seven-slide technical architecture review for staff engineers deciding whether to approve a portable caseflow runtime. Show the invariant lifecycle, adapter boundary, request sequence, benchmark evidence, code contract, failure handling, and decision. Include an editable architecture graph, an editable sequence flow, an editable benchmark chart, and a legible code excerpt. Avoid prose arrows and generic card grids.

briefs.prompt: Create a seven-slide product demo for product leaders evaluating reviewable AI presentation generation. Show the initial brief, structured draft, validation, review-before-accept, trace receipt, editable export, and final decision. Use the supplied journey timestamps. Include a large screenshot or explicit screenshot placeholder with numbered callouts, a before/after comparison, an editable interaction flow, and a trace waterfall. Never claim a screenshot was captured when it was not supplied.

briefs.title: AI Support Entry Strategy
briefs.prompt: Create a seven-slide strategy proposal for product and go-to-market leaders selecting an AI support market-entry wedge. Frame the market tension, compare three segments, show capability fit, sequence the roadmap, surface risks, and recommend one option. Use the supplied scoring table exactly. Include an editable landscape, aligned option comparison, roadmap timeline, and recommendation gate. Avoid generic two-by-two decoration without labeled axes.

briefs.title: How Retrieval-Augmented Generation Works
briefs.decision: understand when retrieval improves an AI product and when it does not
briefs.prompt: Create a seven-slide educational explainer for non-specialist product managers learning when retrieval-augmented generation helps. Progress from the failure of memory-only answers through indexing, retrieval, grounded generation, evaluation, limitations, and a practical decision checklist. Include a progressive editable diagram, a simple timeline of one query, an analogy, and a final summary. Keep technical terms defined in plain language.

attachments.content: The benchmark definition is: index approved documents, retrieve passages relevant to a query, provide those passages to the generator, and evaluate whether claims are supported by the retrieved text. Retrieval does not guarantee truth and can return irrelevant evidence.

briefs.title: Municipal Heat Resilience Options
briefs.prompt: Create a seven-slide policy briefing for city council members choosing a phased heat-resilience package. Show the evidence baseline, affected stakeholders, intervention options, implementation timeline, trade-offs, funding sequence, and recommendation. Use the supplied program facts without claiming measured health outcomes. Include an editable evidence map, stakeholder map, timeline, and trade-off comparison.

briefs.title: Claims Operations Intervention
briefs.decision: decide whether to replicate the intervention in two additional regions
briefs.prompt: Create a seven-slide case study for operations executives deciding whether to replicate a claims-processing intervention. Show the initial state, intervention, adoption path, measured before/after outcomes, operator evidence, limitations, and replication decision. Use the supplied data exactly. Include a before/after comparison, editable process diagram, outcome chart, and evidence panel. Do not imply causality beyond the observed before/after association.

attachments.content: The intervention introduced structured intake, one review checkpoint, and exception routing. The benchmark authorizes an observed before/after association only; it does not establish a causal effect.

briefs.title: A Shipment's Risk Journey
briefs.decision: understand where intervention most reduces shipment risk
briefs.prompt: Create a seven-slide scrollytelling-style deck for supply-chain leaders following one shipment from booking through delivery. Each slide should advance the same journey, reveal one new risk signal, and preserve a coherent static PowerPoint fallback. Use the supplied event sequence exactly. Include an editable progression timeline, cumulative risk chart, handoff diagram, exception evidence, and final intervention decision. Do not fabricate a live interaction or animation in the PPTX.
